metadata.title: Understanding Student Loans
metadata.subtitle: Compare Federal vs. Private Loans and Make Smart Borrowing Decisions

content.title: Understanding Student Loans
content.subtitle: Compare Federal vs. Private Loans and Make Smart Borrowing Decisions

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Alex borrowed $20,000 in federal student loans at 5% fixed interest with income-driven repayment options.

Jordan borrowed $15,000 federal + $10,000 private at 10% variable interest, requiring a parent co-signer.

Both graduated with similar degrees, but their post-graduation financial situations look very different.

What key differences in their loan choices will impact Alex and Jordan for years to come?

objectives.description: Compare federal and private student loans in terms of rates, flexibility, and borrower protections.
Calculate the long-term cost of different student loan scenarios and repayment plans.
Understand repayment options including income-driven plans and loan forgiveness programs.
Develop strategies for borrowing responsibly and minimizing education debt.

terms.definition: Loans from the U.S. Department of Education with fixed rates set by Congress, flexible repayment options, and borrower protections.
terms.example: Direct Subsidized, Direct Unsubsidized, and PLUS loans—rates and terms are the same for all borrowers.

terms.definition: Loans from banks, credit unions, or online lenders. Rates vary based on credit score and typically offer less flexibility.
terms.example: A student with excellent credit might get 6% while fair credit gets 12%—rates are lender and borrower specific.

terms.definition: Federal loan plans that cap monthly payments at a percentage of your discretionary income, with forgiveness after 20-25 years.
terms.example: SAVE, IBR, PAYE, ICR—payments adjust based on income and family size.

terms.definition: Failure to repay student loans as agreed—typically after 270 days of missed payments for federal loans.
terms.example: Default damages credit for years, can result in wage garnishment, tax refund seizure, and even loss of professional licenses.

leftColumn.title: FEDERAL LOANS
items: No credit check for most loans

layoutData.title: Know Your Options
paragraphs: Federal loans come in different types with different terms:
bulletPoints: Direct Subsidized: For undergrads with financial need. Government pays interest while you're in school!
Direct Unsubsidized: Available to all students. Interest accrues while in school.
Direct PLUS Loans: For graduate students or parents. Higher rates, requires credit check.
Direct Consolidation: Combine multiple federal loans into one payment.

keyPoint.text: 💡 Always max out subsidized loans first—free interest while in school is huge!

leftPanel.title: Same Amount, Different Costs
paragraphs: Compare total repayment on $30,000 over 10 years:
Federal (5% fixed):
Monthly: $318 | Total Interest: $8,184
Total Repaid: $38,184
Private (7%, good credit):
Monthly: $348 | Total Interest: $11,760
Total Repaid: $41,760
Private (10%, fair credit):
Monthly: $396 | Total Interest: $17,520
Total Repaid: $47,520

stats.label: Extra cost: fair credit vs. federal

infoCard.title: Variable Rate Risk
items: Private rates can increase over time

leftPanel.title: Payments Based on What You Earn
paragraphs: Federal loans offer several IDR plans:
SAVE Plan: 10% of discretionary income (undergrad) with forgiveness after 20 years
IBR: 10-15% of income, forgiveness after 20-25 years
PAYE: 10% of income, forgiveness after 20 years
ICR: 20% of income or fixed 12-year payment
If your income drops (job loss, lower salary), payments drop too!

highlightBox.text: IDR plans provide a safety net. If you earn $35,000, your payment might be $150/month instead of $300.

stats.label: Possible payment if income is low enough

layoutData.title: Ways to Have Loans Forgiven (Federal Only)
paragraphs: Several programs can eliminate remaining federal loan balances:
bulletPoints: Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF): Work for government or nonprofit for 10 years, make 120 payments, remaining balance forgiven tax-free
IDR Forgiveness: After 20-25 years of payments on income-driven plans, remaining balance forgiven
Teacher Loan Forgiveness: Up to $17,500 forgiven after 5 years teaching in low-income schools
Closed School Discharge: If your school closes while enrolled, loans may be discharged
Total and Permanent Disability: Discharge if you become disabled

keyPoint.text: ⚠️ PSLF requires specific loan types (Direct Loans) and qualifying employers. Verify eligibility BEFORE counting on forgiveness!

leftPanel.title: What Happens If You Don't Pay
paragraphs: Student loan default (270+ days missed) has severe consequences:
Credit damage: Score drops significantly, default stays 7 years
Wage garnishment: Up to 15% of disposable income taken automatically
Tax refund seizure: Federal refunds applied to debt
Social Security garnishment: Can take up to 15% of benefits
Professional license issues: Some states can revoke or deny licenses

highlightBox.text: Unlike most debt, student loans are extremely difficult to discharge in bankruptcy.

infoCard.title: Avoid Default:
items: Explore rehabilitation options if in default

layoutData.title: Minimize What You Need to Borrow
paragraphs: The best student loan is the one you don't need:
bulletPoints: Apply for grants and scholarships first: Free money that doesn't need repayment
Consider in-state public universities: Often 50-70% less than out-of-state or private schools
Work part-time: Even $200/month = $9,600 over 4 years you don't borrow
Start at community college: Complete basics at lower cost, transfer later
Only borrow what you need: Loan refund checks aren't free money
Exhaust federal loans before private: Better rates, better protections

keyPoint.text: 🎯 Rule of thumb: Don't borrow more than your expected first-year salary after graduation.

scenario.name: Alex's Federal-Only Strategy
paragraphs: Alex borrowed $20,000 total in federal loans at 5% fixed. After graduating with a teaching degree, Alex earns $38,000/year.
Alex enrolled in PSLF (public school) and an income-driven plan. Monthly payment: $180 (vs. $212 on standard plan).
After 10 years of public service, remaining balance will be forgiven tax-free.

scenario.name: Jordan's Mixed Strategy
paragraphs: Jordan borrowed $15,000 federal + $10,000 private at 10% variable. The private loan required Jordan's parent as co-signer.
After graduating, Jordan earns $40,000. The private loan rate increased to 12%. Jordan must make two separate payments.
No income-driven option for private loans. If Jordan loses their job, the private payment stays the same.

content.question:
Compare Alex and Jordan's situations:

1. Who has more financial flexibility if they lose their job? Why?

2. Why is the co-signer requirement on Jordan's private loan a potential problem?

3. How much more will Jordan pay over 10 years due to the higher private loan rate?

If you plan to attend college, what is your strategy for financing your education?

What steps can you take NOW to minimize how much you'll need to borrow?

questions.question: Which type of student loan offers income-driven repayment plans and potential loan forgiveness?
Why should you exhaust federal loan options before considering private loans?
A student borrows $20,000 at 5% for 10 years ($212/month). What's the total repaid and total interest?
List two consequences of defaulting on student loans.

takeaways.title: Federal First, Always
takeaways.description: Federal loans offer fixed rates, income-driven plans, and forgiveness options. Exhaust these before considering private.

takeaways.title: Know Your Repayment Options
takeaways.description: Income-driven repayment can lower payments when income is low. PSLF can forgive loans after 10 years of public service.

takeaways.title: Private Loans Have Fewer Protections
takeaways.description: Variable rates can increase, no income-driven plans, no forgiveness, and often require co-signers.

takeaways.title: Borrow Only What You Need
takeaways.description: Don't borrow more than expected first-year salary. Grants, scholarships, and work-study reduce borrowing.

main.title: Student Loan Strategy Builder
main.description: Alex and Jordan made different choices—but what would YOU do? Tomorrow you'll analyze loan scenarios, compare costs, and create a personal education financing strategy.

steps: Compare federal vs. private loan features for real scenarios
Calculate total costs under different interest rates and terms
Determine eligibility for income-driven repayment and forgiveness programs
Analyze the impact of variable vs. fixed interest rates
Create a personal strategy to minimize education debt

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
